text: The surgeon begins robotic dissection over the sacral promontory, aiming to identify and clean the periosteum for suture pexy. The iliac veins are visible crossing the area, and the sacral bone is exposed. The redundancy of the tissue is examined, which can complicate the procedure.

The surgeon begins the robotic proctopexy by docking the robotic arms in a transverse orientation. Dissection is initiated over the sacral promontory, with the periosteum being carefully cleaned for future suture pexy. The iliac veins are visible crossing the area, and the sacral bone is exposed. The surgeon examines the redundant tissue, which can complicate the procedure.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection in the retrorectal space, frequently readjusting due to excessive tissue redundancy. The robotic setup is adjusted to two right arms and one left, positioning the camera centrally to facilitate access to the pelvis. The scissors in arm three are used for precise dissection without interference from the iliac vessels.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection in the retrorectal space, frequently readjusting due to excessive tissue redundancy. The camera is centrally positioned, facilitating the right-sided dissection with arm three, equipped with scissors, reaching into the pelvis without obstructing the iliac vessels.

The surgeon navigates the retrorectal space, progressing towards the coccyx, while addressing camera fogging issues due to interstitial edema. The view is adjusted to a thirty-degree upward angle for better visualization in the deep pelvis. The surgeon then transitions to anterior dissection within the rectovaginal septum.

The surgeon navigates the retrorectal space towards the coccyx, addressing camera fogging issues due to interstitial edema. The view is adjusted to a thirty-degree upward angle for better visualization in the deep pelvis. The surgeon then switches to anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum, encountering minor bleeding while assessing the depth of the dissection without fully opening the septum.

The surgeon encounters minor bleeding while dissecting in the rectovaginal septum. The dissection is continued to open the septum further, followed by completing the right-sided dissection and connecting it to the posterior dissection.

The surgeon completes right-sided dissection, connecting it to the posterior dissection. Despite camera fogging due to interstitial edema, the surgeon lifts the rectum off the coccyx. The dissection proceeds circumferentially around the posterior along the right side, followed by deep anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum. The vagina is elevated off the rectum using robotic arm number four, extending the dissection down to the perineal body.
The surgeon lifts the rectum off the coccyx, completing the right-sided dissection despite camera fogging issues caused by interstitial edema.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection around the posterior and right side, followed by deep anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum. The vagina is visibly lifted off the rectum using robotic arm number four, extending the dissection down to the perineal body.

The surgeon prepares to place rectopexy sutures after reaching the perineal floor. The cleaned periosteum is visible, allowing for secure suture placement without risk of bleeding. The lateral stalks and peritoneum are pulled under tension as the surgeon takes large bites with tool silk, placing two right-sided sutures.

The surgeon places rectopexy sutures on the cleaned periosteum at the perineal floor, pulling lateral stalks and peritoneum under tension for secure bites using tool silk. After completing the pexy, the peritoneal opening is closed to prevent small bowel from dropping and forming adhesions or an internal hernia.
The surgeon completes the rectopexy and proceeds to close the peritoneal opening, ensuring that the small bowel does not drop into the space, which could lead to adhesions or an internal hernia.

The surgeon closes the flap to exclude the mesh from the peritoneal cavity, preventing contact between the mesh and small bowel or other viscera. The uterus is lowered, and hemostasis is achieved at the needle holes anteriorly and posteriorly. A final inspection of the surgical site is performed.
